message.content:
Differentiator titles and descriptions:
1. Mechanistic-Research Framing: High participants interpret their experiences through neurochemistry, clinical research, and literature rather than everyday feeling language. They frequently reference neurotransmitters (serotonin, dopamine), drug classes (SSRIs, psilocybin, MDMA), specific molecules (CB2), and researchers/institutions (Shulgin, NIH, MAPS), and even societal forces (capitalism’s role in psychedelics). This scientific lens shapes their narratives, questions, and expectations during tasks. In contrast, low participants largely describe sensations, relationships, and personal contexts with minimal mechanistic or scholarly framing.

2. Meta-Task Inference and Pattern-Seeking: High participants actively hypothesize hidden motives, experimental manipulations, and patterns beyond task instructions. They notice order changes, question whether buttons have purpose, infer “psychological flexibility” tests, and see nonrandom contingencies (e.g., staff origin coincidences), often labeling this as “not negative paranoia.” Low participants generally engage the tasks at face value, noting performance issues (missed items, visual reflections) without building layered, ulterior-test theories.

3. Intentional Self-Regulation via Breathing and Body Checks: High participants regularly deploy explicit strategies (breath control, releasing tension, cognitive refocusing) to manage anxiety and maintain performance. They ground themselves, monitor bodily tension (e.g., toes curling), and frame difficult moments as valleys to navigate through controlled breathing and attention. Low participants more often report being overwhelmed, toggling between “feeling” and “thinking,” or drifting focus, without naming concrete, repeatable regulation techniques.

4. Empathic Social Attunement with Boundary Awareness: High participants emphasize outward empathy, mirroring others, and craving connection and touch, while simultaneously monitoring appropriateness and others’ comfort. They discuss “the empathy drug,” bonding intentions, and refrain from hugs to respect boundaries and social roles, often checking whether interactions might make others uncomfortable. Low participants are certainly emotional and relational, but their focus centers more on their own feelings, confidentiality concerns, and not imposing, with less real-time boundary calibration and mirroring.

5. Abstract, System-Level Analogy and Philosophy: High participants generalize personal experiences into broad analogies and system-level reflections (e.g., depression as “bad trips,” breathing as agency under extreme historical hardship, commercialization of psychedelics, transcendent unity vs. dissociation). They connect the session to cognitive biases (Kahneman), policy, and societal control of drugs. Low participants tend to stay concrete and localized—work routines, family dynamics, bodily sensations—rarely elaborating into historical, philosophical, or macro-level frames.

Differentiator variable names:
scientific_mechanistic_reasoning, meta_task_inference, breath_based_self_regulation, empathic_attunement_boundaries, abstract_system_level_framing
